Current State:
Current State:
Common examiner pitfalls and fixes:

1) Poor observation (mirrors and blind spots)
- Why it fails: late or no mirror checks lead to unsafe lane changes, poor gap selection and missed hazards.
- Fix: make mirror checks a habit: before any change of speed or direction, do quick mirror–signal–manoeuvre checks. Practice deliberately counting mirror checks on short drives.

2) Inadequate shoulder checks / blind‑spot neglect
- Why: mirrors don’t show everything; missing a shoulder check can be a serious fault.
- Fix: practise the shoulder‑check routine on every lane change and turn until it’s automatic. Use cones or recorded sessions to check consistency.

3) Poor judgement at junctions (pulling out, gap selection)
- Why: entering a junction with insufficient gap or without full observation is frequently marked as a serious fault.
- Fix: rehearse the “look, look again, decide” routine; on supervised runs, have your supervisor call out whether a gap is safe before you commit.

4) Incorrect or inconsistent use of signals
- Why: wrong, late, or forgotten signals confuse other road users and examiners penalise it.
- Fix: signal earlier and for long enough; practise signalling with every lane change and turn even in quiet conditions.

5) Wrong road position and lane discipline
- Why: cutting corners, straddling lanes, or being too close to kerbs shows poor positioning.
- Fix: practise correct lane entry/exit lines and mid‑lane positioning on multi‑lane roads; use reference points and instructor feedback.

6) Inappropriate speed (too fast or too slow)
- Why: speed that doesn’t match road conditions earns faults and reduces reaction time.
- Fix: train with a focus on speed judgement: pick markers to judge safe speeds, and practice 3–4 seconds following distance.

7) Hesitation and indecision (overcaution)
- Why: excessive hesitation at junctions or roundabouts can be treated as an inability to drive independently.
- Fix: graded exposure to busier roads; practice making and committing to safe, timely decisions with an instructor.

8) Poor clutch/gear control (manual)
- Why: stalling frequently, inappropriate gear choice, or jerky gear changes lower the examiner’s confidence in your control.
- Fix: do repetitive bite‑point, hill‑start and gear‑change drills until smooth and automatic.

9) Faults in manoeuvres (parallel park, reverse, three‑point turn)
- Why: repeated corrections, hitting kerbs or unsafe reversals are test‑critical.
- Fix: break each manoeuvre into steps, practice 20–50 repetitions with cones, then transition to real cars only when consistent.

10) Cutting corners or wrong turning technique on roundabouts
- Why: poor steering control or entering the wrong lane is a common serious fault.
- Fix: practise correct entry/exit lanes and steering lines for roundabouts of different sizes.

11) Poor observation while moving off or changing lanes
- Why: moving off into traffic without full checks or changing lanes without checking blind spots is unsafe.
- Fix: always pause briefly, check mirrors, quick shoulder check, then move; rehearse this ritual on every move‑off.

12) Failing to follow examiner instructions or misreading independent driving tasks
- Why: missing route directions or not following navigation instructions looks like poor concentration.
- Fix: practise following satnav directions and road signs; if unsure, repeat the examiner’s instruction back before starting.

13) Distractions and poor interior management
- Why: fumbling with controls, phone, or maps distracts you from road critical tasks.
- Fix: do a pre‑drive checklist to set mirrors, music

Mock test preparation and feedback focus

Two weeks before the real test is a practical timeframe for a mock test.
- Aim: run a mock test that mirrors real test conditions as closely as possible to yield meaningful feedback.

What to do during the mock
- Simulate exam conditions exactly
  - No coaching during the drive (instructor acts like an examiner).
  - Use the same paperwork, test‑car setup and arrival routine you’ll use on test day.
  - Start with a short, realistic pre‑drive check just like the examiner would ask.

- Include a full test‑length route and all likely elements
  - Independent driving section (satnav/signs) for the appropriate time/distance.
  - At least one of each manoeuvre you might be asked to do (parallel park, bay/angle park, hill start, three‑point turn, reversing around a corner) — emphasise parallel park because you’re worried about it.
  - Several junctions, multi‑lane roundabouts and at least one slip‑road/short motorway stretch if your test covers high‑speed roads.
  - One or two lane changes and a sustained drive segment to test speed and following distance.

- Recreate test pressure and constraints
  - Time your mock and treat faults as real (use the same minor/serious fail thresholds your local test uses).
  - No practice immediately before the mock; arrive as you would for the real test.
  - Ask the instructor to be strict and mark objectively.

What to have the instructor focus on for feedback
- The concrete, repeatable faults: mirror/shoulder check frequency, late/absent signals, poor gap judgement at junctions, incorrect lane choice at roundabouts, speed selection, and clutch/gear control (manual).
- Manoeuvre specifics: exact reference points used, steering/turning errors, kerb touches, and how many adjustments were needed.
- Examiner‑style faults: identify anything that would count as a serious/fail and why.
- Practical corrective drills: a small number (1–3) of specific drills you can repeat to fix the top faults.

How to get the most useful feedback
- Request both a verbal debrief and a written/recorded list of faults immediately afterwards.
- Record the drive (phone dash or passenger camera) so you can review mirror checks, shoulder checks, and positioning.
- Ask the instructor to demonstrate the exact correction and then watch you do it once in the same session.
- If possible, do a second, short run immediately after the debrief focused only on the top 1–2 faults to check for quick improvement.

Scoring and fault logging
- Treat the mock as a pass/fail with clear thresholds (e.g., “no recurring serious faults; only occasional minor faults allowed”).
- Log faults in a simple sheet: date, fault category, example, corrective drill, times repeated. Use that log to prioritise the next two weeks.

Practical immediate next steps after the mock (brief)
- Prioritise the top 2 recurring/serious faults and schedule targeted lessons/drills for them.
- Book one short pre‑test tune lesson 2–3 days before the real test if possible (a calm, focused session).
- Leave the day before the test for light practice only and rest.

Given your concerns, ask the instructor running the mock to emphasise parallel parking, motorway merges/lanes and observation/shoulder checks so those get especially strict marking.
